content.text: Self-Service Environment Provisioning in IDP

Self-service environment provisioning transforms how GenAI teams spin up development, staging, and test infrastructure. Instead of waiting days for platform tickets to be resolved, engineers request a fully-configured AI workspace from the Internal Developer Portal (IDP) and receive GPU nodes, managed LLM gateway credentials, object storage, and a registered Backstage catalog entry within minutes — all governed by policy and cost guardrails applied automatically at request time.

This sub-subtopic covers how to design, implement, and operate a GenAI-aware self-service provisioning system built on Backstage Software Templates, Terraform, Open Policy Agent (OPA), and GitOps pipelines.
Why GenAI Environments Differ from Standard Cloud Workloads

Conventional web service environments need compute, a database, and a load balancer. GenAI environments add several orthogonal dimensions that a generic self-service platform cannot handle without explicit support:
Dimension
Standard Workload
GenAI Workload
Compute
CPU VM or container
GPU node pool (A10G, H100); preemptible vs reserved
External APIs
None or minor
LLM gateway with per-team token budgets + rate limits
Data handling
Relational DB
Vector store, embedding cache, model artefact bucket
Cost volatility
Predictable $/h
Unpredictable (GPU burst, token costs); needs hard caps
Compliance
Standard PII rules
AI-specific: model provenance, eval dataset handling
Expiry
Long-lived
Short-lived sandboxes; auto-expire to prevent GPU waste
A self-service platform designed for GenAI must encode these requirements into the provisioning templates themselves — not leave them as documentation for engineers to follow manually.
IDP Architecture for Environment Provisioning
Backstage Software Templates

Backstage Software Templates are the user-facing entry point. Each template represents an environment class (sandbox, staging, perf, prod-like) and collects structured input from the developer through a UI form. The template then triggers a scaffolding action that creates a GitOps PR.

# catalog/templates/genai-sandbox.yaml
apiVersion: scaffolder.backstage.io/v1beta3
kind: Template
metadata:
  name: genai-sandbox
  title: GenAI Sandbox Environment
  tags: [genai, sandbox, gpu]
spec:
  owner: platform-team
  type: environment
  parameters:
    - title: Environment Details
      required: [name, owner]
      properties:
        name:
          type: string
          pattern: '^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,30}$'
        owner:
          type: string
          ui:field: OwnerPicker
        gpuClass:
          type: string
          default: a10g
          enum: [none, a10g, h100]
        llmModels:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
            enum: [gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet, gemini-1.5-pro, llama-3-70b]
          uniqueItems: true
        durationDays:
          type: integer
          default: 7
          minimum: 1
          maximum: 14
  steps:
    - id: validate-quota
      name: Validate GPU quota
      action: http:backstage:request
      input:
        method: POST
        path: /api/platform/quota/check
        body: { team: "${{ parameters.owner }}", gpuClass: "${{ parameters.gpuClass }}" }

    - id: render-terraform
      name: Render Terraform variables
      action: fetch:template
      input:
        url: ./terraform-templates/sandbox
        values:
          name: ${{ parameters.name }}
          owner: ${{ parameters.owner }}
          gpuClass: ${{ parameters.gpuClass }}
          llmModels: ${{ parameters.llmModels }}
          ttlDays: ${{ parameters.durationDays }}

    - id: open-pr
      name: Open provisioning PR
      action: publish:github:pull-request
      input:
        repoUrl: github.com/myorg/infra-gitops
        title: "env: provision genai-sandbox/${{ parameters.name }}"
        branchName: env/sandbox-${{ parameters.name }}

  output:
    links:
      - title: Provisioning PR
        url: ${{ steps['open-pr'].output.remoteUrl }}
GitOps Pipeline and Terraform Execution

The PR opened by the Backstage template lands in an infra GitOps repository. A CI pipeline runs plan → OPA policy check → apply. The split between plan and apply allows a policy gate to inspect the Terraform plan in machine-readable JSON before any cloud resources are created.

# .github/workflows/env-provision.yml
name: Environment Provisioning
on:
  pull_request:  { paths: ['environments/**'] }
  push:          { branches: [main], paths: ['environments/**'] }

jobs:
  plan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs: { plan_json: "${{ steps.plan.outputs.json }}" }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - name: Terraform Plan (JSON)
        id: plan
        run: |
          terraform init && terraform plan -out=tfplan.bin
          terraform show -json tfplan.bin > plan.json
          echo "json=$(base64 -w0 plan.json)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        working-directory: environments/${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}

  policy-check:
    needs: plan
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: open-policy-agent/setup-opa@v2
      - run: |
          echo "${{ needs.plan.outputs.plan_json }}" | base64 -d > plan.json
          opa eval --data policies/genai-env.rego --input plan.json             'data.genai.env.deny' --fail-defined

  apply:
    needs: [plan, policy-check]
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve
        working-directory: environments/${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
      - name: Register in Backstage catalog
        run: |
          curl -sX POST https://backstage.internal/api/catalog/locations \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.BACKSTAGE_TOKEN }}" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{"type":"url","target":"https://github.com/myorg/infra-gitops/blob/main/environments/${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}/catalog-info.yaml"}'
OPA Policy Enforcement for GenAI Environments
Open Policy Agent evaluates the Terraform plan JSON before apply. GenAI-specific policies enforce GPU budget caps, model allowlists, mandatory tagging, and data classification constraints.
LLM Gateway Integration at Provisioning Time

Each environment receives a scoped LLM Gateway credential automatically injected during provisioning. The gateway enforces per-environment token budgets independently of per-team budgets, ensuring that a runaway sandbox cannot exhaust production allowances.
Environment Lifecycle and Auto-Expiry
TTL Tracking and Teardown
Every provisioned environment carries a
ttl-date

tag. A nightly expiry controller runs as a Kubernetes CronJob. It lists all environments whose TTL has elapsed and triggers Terraform destroy via the GitOps pipeline, then de-registers the catalog entry from Backstage.
Cost Visibility and Budget Alerts

Each environment's cloud spend is tracked in real time. The platform emits a daily cost report per environment tag and sends a Slack alert when 80% of the environment's daily GPU budget is consumed — allowing developers to either extend the budget or shut down the environment before overage charges appear.

# platform/cost/reporter.py
import httpx
from google.cloud import billing_v1

def compute_env_cost(project: str, env_tag: str, date_str: str) -> float:
    """Return USD spend for a single environment on a given date."""
    client = billing_v1.CloudBillingClient()
    # Use BigQuery export; simplified here for clarity
    from google.cloud import bigquery
    bq = bigquery.Client(project=project)
    query = f"""
        SELECT SUM(cost) as total
        FROM `{project}.billing_export.gcp_billing_export_v1_*`
        WHERE DATE(usage_start_time) = '{date_str}'
          AND labels.key = 'env-id'
          AND labels.value = '{env_tag}'
    """
    rows = list(bq.query(query).result())
    return float(rows[0].total or 0)

def alert_if_approaching_limit(env_id: str, spend: float, daily_limit: float) -> None:
    pct = spend / daily_limit
    if pct >= 0.8:
        httpx.post(
            SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL,
            json={
                "text": (
                    f":warning: Environment *{env_id}* has consumed "
                    f"{pct:.0%} of its ${daily_limit:.0f}/day budget "
                    f"(${spend:.2f} today). Consider extending the budget or "
                    "shutting down unused workloads."
                )
            },
        )
Terraform Module Structure for GenAI Environments
A reusable Terraform module encapsulates all resources needed for a standard GenAI environment. Backstage templates render a
terraform.tfvars
file and call this module — platform teams maintain the module, application teams never touch the underlying resource definitions.
Catalog-Info Auto-Generation
After Terraform apply completes, the CI pipeline writes a
catalog-info.yaml

file into the GitOps repository and registers it with the Backstage catalog. This means every provisioned environment is immediately visible in the IDP with its owner, GPU class, cost, and TTL — no manual catalog updates required.

# catalog-info.yaml  (auto-generated after Terraform apply)
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Resource
metadata:
  name: env-team-alpha-sandbox-8f3a
  title: "GenAI Env: team-alpha-sandbox-8f3a"
  description: "Self-service GenAI sandbox for team-alpha"
  annotations:
    backstage.io/managed-by-location: "url:https://github.com/myorg/infra-gitops"
    cloud.google.com/cluster-name: genai-team-alpha-sandbox-8f3a
  labels:
    env-type: sandbox
    gpu-class: a10g
    ttl-date: "2026-07-08"
  tags: [genai, sandbox, gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet]
spec:
  type: genai-environment
  owner: group:default/team-alpha
  system: genai-platform
  dependsOn:
    - resource:default/llm-gateway
    - resource:default/vector-store
  x-genai:
    gpuClass: a10g
    modelsAllowed: [gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet]
    artefactBucket: genai-env-team-alpha-sandbox-8f3a-artefacts
    expiresOn: "2026-07-08"
Common Pitfalls and Operational Considerations
Pitfall
Symptom
Fix
GPU quota not pre-checked
Apply fails mid-run, partial infra left orphaned
Run quota check in Backstage template step before opening the PR
Missing TTL tag
Environments never expire; GPU costs accumulate silently
OPA deny rule blocks any plan lacking ttl-date label
LLM creds stored in Git
Secret rotation breaks all envs simultaneously
Inject credentials via External Secrets Operator referencing Vault/Secret Manager
No cost alerting
Team unaware of runaway GPU usage until billing cycle
Daily spend check CronJob + Slack alert at 80% threshold
Catalog entry not de-registered
Dead entries clutter the IDP catalog
Teardown pipeline calls Backstage delete API as a required final step
Identical env names
Terraform state collision between teams
Name template: {team}-{env_type}-{random_suffix}; enforce uniqueness in OPA
Testing the Provisioning Pipeline

Platform teams must validate the provisioning pipeline itself, not just the applications it hosts. The following test suite verifies the end-to-end flow in a CI environment using Terraform's test framework.
Summary
Self-service environment provisioning in an IDP eliminates the ticket-and-wait bottleneck for GenAI teams. The key design decisions are:
Backstage Software Templates as the typed, validated user interface — engineers fill in a form, not a wiki page
GitOps as the execution layer — all provisioning is a PR, giving full audit trail and rollback capability
OPA policy gate between plan and apply — GPU allowlists, budget caps, and mandatory tagging enforced automatically
Scoped LLM Gateway credentials injected at provisioning time — sandboxes cannot exhaust production token budgets
TTL-based auto-expiry — nightly controller tears down expired environments and de-registers them from the catalog
Automatic catalog entry creation — every environment is immediately visible in the IDP with its owner, resources, and expiry date

Together these mechanisms let a GenAI engineer have a fully-configured GPU workspace within minutes, while keeping the platform team confident that costs, compliance, and catalog accuracy are maintained without manual intervention.
